CalifOrnia
Fair P01itical
Practices Commission
suzanne L. GIad
Deputy Director for External
California tluseun of science
700 State Drive
Los Angeles, CA 9003 7
Dear Ms. Glad:
This letter is in response to your re![uest for advice under
the provisions of the Politica} Refom Act (the "Acttr).r 
Because
your request for advice is of a general nature and seeks general
gruidelines, we treat your letter as a request for inforBal as-
sistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c) (copy enclosed).2
we do not provide advice concerning provisions of law other than
the Act. You nay wish to consult rrith the Attorney General's of-
fice regarding other provisions of 1aw such as the California
constitution which prohibits the gift of public funds.
OUESTION
For purposes of the disclosure provisions of the Act, what is
the appropriate valuation of a gift of a parking Pass given to a
public officiaL?
CONCLUSTON
For purposes of the disclosure provisions of the Act, the
value of a gift of a parking pass is the fair market value of the
actual use of the pass by the officiaf 
and by any other user of
the pass. However, if the pass is issued for and used by the
official exclusively in connection with the public official's
official business, disclosure is not required.
1 Go*r".rrr"nt Code Sections 81OOO-91015. AlL statutory references
are to the covernment Code unless otherwise indicated. Corunission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18o0o, g! seo. A11 references to regulations are to Tit1e 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Infornal assistance cloes not
innunity provided by an opinion
tion 83114 i Regnrlation 18329(c)
April 4, 199 0
Re:
Affairs
and Industry
Your Request for Infornal Assistance
Our File }{o. I-90-14 8
provide the requestor r,ith the
or formal uritten advice. (Sec-
(3) . )
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 958O4-OEOZ . (9f 6)322-5660


File No. I-90-■48
Page 2
FACTS
The california l{useum of Science and Industry (the museum), a
public entity, issues free parking passes for its courtesy parking
1ot. The passes are issued each Augrust for the following year and
are distributed to nuseum staff, donors, corporate sponsors of
exhibits, volunteers and public officials. 
In order to receive a
free parking pass, donors nust contribute S550 to the museum.
Corporations rrhich contribute S25,000 also receive free parking
passes. The passes issued to public officials do not show the
name of the public official to whom the passes have been issued.
The rnuseum parking lot is adj acent to a sports arena where a
wide range of events takes place. The parking 1ot is available to
nembers of the public who visit the museum or attend events at the
sports arena. llenbers of the general publj,c who wish to use the
parking 1ot nust pay $1 on rreek days and 55 on weekends.
You have received inquiries froro legislators who wish to
deterrnine the value of the parking pass for purposes of disclosure
in their annual financial disclosure statenents. In the future,
you rrish to provide aII public officials sho receive free parking
passes rrith this infornation.
ANALYSTS
Section 87207 (a) (1) requires that public officials report the
narne and address of each source of gifts with an aggregate value
of $50 or more within a calendar year. Section 82028(a) defines
trgiftri as:
... any palment to the extent that consideration of
equal or greater value is not recej.ved and includes
a rebate or discount in the price of anything of
value unless the rebate or discount is roade in the
regarlar course of business to uembers of the public
without regard to official 
status.
section 82028 (a) .
The parking passes are not available to meDbers of the general
public. Thus, to the extent that the museum does not receive
consideration of equal or greater value fron the public official
who receives a parking pass, the pass constitutes a gift to the
public official.
The Act requires public off icial.s to file annual financial
disclosure stateuents disclosing among other things gifts of g50
or more received during the previous calendar year. (Sections
87203 and A72O7 i Regnrlation L8723, copy enclosed. ) For purposes
of disclosure, the value of a gift pass is the fair uarket value
of the actual use of _the pass by the official, 
including gTuests
aceompanying the official, 
plus the fair narket value oi iny use
by any person or persons to whorn the official transfers the


File No. I-9 0-148
Page 3
privilege of the use of the pass. (Reg'ulation La726.3(a), copy
enclosed. )
In the present situation, the fair market value of the park-
ing pass is the price which a person would otherrrise have to pay
to use the parking Iot. 
You have stated in your request for
advice that a member of the public who wishes to use the parking
Iot uust pay 91 on rreek days and 95 on rreekends. Therefore, when
the pass is used on week days, the value is $1 per use. On
weekends, the value of the pass is $5 per use. Thus, for report-
ing purposes, the value of the pass is the cumulation of the
actual use of the parking pass by the public official 
and by
anyone else to rrhom the official transfers the privilege of using
the pass during the calendar year.3
However, we have previously advised that rrhen the parking
pass is j.ssued for use while on official business, such as
legislative ueetings held at the museum, the pubLic official 
need
not disclose the receipt of the pass. (In re Thomas (1977) 3 FPPC
ops. 30, copy enclosed. ) Therefore, if a parking pass is issued
to a public official 
and the official 
uses the pass for official
business only, no reportinq obligations are incurred. If the pass
is used for personal purposes unrelated to official duties, then
such uses for personal purposes should be valued and disclosed as
discussed above.
Should you have any further questions regarding this natter,
do not hesitate to contact De at (916) 322-59OL.
sincerely,
Kathrlm E. Donovan
ceneral Counsel
KED:BMB:p■h
Enc■osures
町  1シ
Counsel, Legal Division
3 Since the value of the pass is based on actuaL use, an official
rrho never uses the pass, nor transfers the privilege of using the
pass to someone else, has no reporting obligationsi that pariing
pass has no value for purposes of disclosure. (Regulation
18726.3 (a) . )
